interpretation_summary: The replication successfully executed a Python translation of the original analysis pipeline and ran the preregistered one-sample Wilcoxon signed-rank test comparing group-round mean market price to the fixed value 1.94. The code produced group-round aggregates and returned a two-tailed Wilcoxon p = 0.029 with a mean group-round price = 1.9063 (sd = 1.0801). This outcome differs from the original reported result (Wilcoxon p = 0.001 and stated direction UNIFORM > FIXED). Overall fidelity to the planned analysis is high for the core test (same nonparametric test on the same derived outcome), but there are important deviations in missing-value/subset handling (dropout/bankrupt flags were placeholders) and in the sample/variable mapping (some task_type values are blank and the available group counts differ from those reported in the paper). These deviations plausibly explain at least part of the discrepancy between original and replication p-values and direction.

fidelity_assessment.method_alignment: High alignment for the primary test: the replication reshaped raw round CSVs to group-round means and ran a two-tailed Wilcoxon signed-rank test (price vs 1.94) as preregistered. Aggregation and the choice of nonparametric test follow the preregistered plan. Minor-to-moderate deviations exist in handling dropouts/bankrupts (implemented as placeholders) and in variable mapping (some treatment/task_type values were not propagated), which affect robustness/subset tests and ability to run treatment-stratified checks.

deviations.issue_description: Dropout and bankrupt indicators were implemented as placeholders (dropout=0, bankrupt=0) rather than computed from the raw rounds_missed variables per the original .do.
deviations.impact: Moderate: This makes the subset tests (non-dropouts; non-dropouts & non-bankrupt) identical to the full-sample test and prevents faithful replication of robustness checks. It could materially affect p-values reported for those subsets.

deviations.issue_description: Sample/derived data counts differ from the numbers reported in the original paper (original reported ~28 valid groups; provided/derived files contain different group counts). The executed run shows inconsistent N reported for different outputs (test n reported as 792; mean reported on N=880).
deviations.impact: Moderate-to-High: Differences in which groups/rounds are included or in how missing rows were handled can change the test statistic and p-value and thus affect whether results match the original.

deviations.issue_description: Task_type (treatment: 'UNIFORM' vs 'FIXED') appears not to be fully populated in the derived file (many blank values), limiting ability to reproduce treatment-stratified comparisons and confirm that the observed difference corresponds to the same treatment contrast as the original.
deviations.impact: Low-to-Moderate: Prevents direct verification of the original directional claim (UNIFORM > FIXED) in treatment-specific tests even though the preregistered focal test was the one-sample comparison to 1.94.

results_comparison.hypothesis_tested: One-sample Wilcoxon signed-rank test: whether the group-round mean market-clearing price differs from fixed price 1.94 (two-tailed). Focal substantive claim: UNIFORM average market price > FIXED (i.e., prices in uniform treatment exceed the fixed price 1.94).
results_comparison.original_results: Reported in initial materials: Wilcoxon signed-rank test gave p = 0.001 (two-tailed) and the paper states that UNIFORM produced higher market prices than FIXED (direction: UNIFORM > FIXED). The original summary thus finds a statistically significant and substantively higher price under UNIFORM relative to the fixed benchmark.
results_comparison.replication_results: Replication (Python translation) results: Wilcoxon statistic = 143025.5 (as printed by the script), p = 0.029071026187066935 (two-tailed). Mean group-round price = 1.9063 (sd = 1.0801). Mean difference = 1.9063 - 1.94 = -0.0337 (i.e., slightly below 1.94). Approx effect size (Cohen's d) ≈ -0.031. Reported sample sizes from execution: Wilcoxon test n = 792; descriptive mean reported on N = 880 (this mismatch in N is a noted deviation requiring resolution).
results_comparison.overall_answer: No — The replication does not support the original preregistered comparison in the same way. While the replication produced a nominally significant two-tailed p-value (p ≈ 0.029), the observed mean is slightly below 1.94 (opposite the reported direction) and the p-value is much less extreme than the original p = 0.001. Given the sample differences and the placeholder handling of dropouts/bankrupts, the replication fails to reproduce the original result as reported.

replication_report: Replication executed successfully, but it did not reproduce the original focal finding. The group-round mean price in the provided data is 1.9063 (sd = 1.0801) and yields a Wilcoxon two-tailed p ≈ 0.029 versus 1.94; this is smaller in magnitude and opposite in sign to the original claim that UNIFORM produced significantly higher prices (original p = 0.001). Deviation in missing-data/subset handling and sample composition likely contributed to the discrepancy.

failure_handling.suggestions: Provide the exact processed group-round CSVs used by the original paper (the derived fullsample_analysis.csv/round1_analysis.csv that the authors used), or provide documentation mapping raw columns to the derived variables (explicitly which groups/rounds were excluded and how rounds_missed/bankrupt were computed). Confirm original sample counts (number of groups and group-round rows) so we can align sample filters exactly.

Replace the placeholder dropout/bankrupt logic with the original calculation (use rounds_missed variables to compute dropout and the original bankrupt indicator logic) as in replication_analysis.do. Alternatively, run the provided Stata .do in a Stata environment (or run it with StatTransfer-like fidelity) to compare outputs and identify differences introduced in the Python translation.

Ensure task_type/treatment is parsed and propagated correctly so treatment-stratified tests and directional comparisons (UNIFORM vs FIXED) can be run. Re-run tests after correcting data mapping to verify directionality and p-values. Document any remaining deviations from the original analytical choices (e.g., handling of NA prices, per-round aggregation rules).

Resolve the reported mismatch in sample counts (Wilcoxon n vs descriptive N). Re-run the script with additional logging: report counts at each transformation step (number of group-round rows before/after NA drop, counts per group, counts used in the Wilcoxon). Save intermediate datasets used for each test so outputs can be inspected.

notes: Caveats and further recommendations: (1) The observed effect size is extremely small (mean difference ≈ -0.0337; Cohen's d ≈ -0.03), so even when p<0.05 the practical difference is negligible and sensitive to small sample/data-handling changes. (2) Because dropout/bankrupt subsetting was not implemented faithfully, robustness checks reported in the original cannot be assessed; implementing these flags may change p-values and direction. (3) If the original finding depends on excluding certain groups/rounds or on treatment-specific aggregation, differences in group counts or treatment labeling could easily change the result from highly significant (p=0.001) to non-significant or reversed. (4) Recommended next steps: (a) implement correct dropout/bankrupt computation per the Stata .do and re-run; (b) compare Python-derived aggregates to outputs produced by the original Stata .do (the provided .do file in replication_data) to isolate translation/data-mapping differences; (c) request the original processed CSVs or a short note from the authors clarifying any data cleaning steps that were not represented in the repository.
